content.text: The Problem Hooks Were Designed to Solve

Hooks are functions that let you use React state and lifecycle features inside function components. They were introduced in React 16.8 (February 2019) to address a cluster of problems that had accumulated in the class component model. Understanding
hooks exist makes the individual hook APIs much easier to reason about.

attrs.alt: Before vs after hooks: class components vs function components

content.text: By 2018 the React team had shipped class components for five years and watched patterns emerge across thousands of codebases. Three problems stood out as foundational. They were not minor ergonomic complaints — they were structural flaws in how state and side-effects could be composed and shared.

Extracting stateful logic from a class component for reuse required wrapping the component in a higher-order component (HOC) or using a render prop. Both approaches work, but they create an artificial component hierarchy in the DevTools tree and make the data flow harder to trace:
Custom hooks solve this without wrapper components. Logic that was previously entangled in a HOC or render prop becomes an ordinary function you import:
Custom hooks are the primary way to extract and share stateful logic between components. Unlike HOCs and render props, they do not change the component hierarchy and they compose without nesting.
Problem 2: Splitting Related Logic Across Lifecycle Methods
Class lifecycle methods forced you to mix unrelated logic in the same method and split related logic across different methods.
tear down the subscription. The subscription setup and its cleanup lived in two different places:
puts setup and cleanup in the same function, and lets you have as many effects as you like — one per concern:
Classes introduce several sources of confusion that go beyond syntax. The
keyword behaves differently in JavaScript than in most object-oriented languages — handlers must be bound explicitly or written as arrow class properties. Constructors must call
Function components with hooks eliminate all of this. There is no
, no constructor, no binding. The component is just a function that returns JSX.
A hook is a function whose name starts with
. React's built-in hooks follow this convention:
, and so on. Custom hooks follow the same convention and can call other hooks internally. The
prefix is the signal to React — and to the ESLint plugin — that this function must follow the

Hooks work by attaching state to the Fiber associated with the component instance. Each call to a hook during a render appends to a linked list of hook records stored on the Fiber. The order in which hooks are called must be identical on every render so React can pair each hook call with its stored record. This is why you cannot call hooks inside conditionals or loops.
React ships a set of built-in hooks covering the four categories of concerns that previously required class lifecycle methods:

Any function that calls built-in hooks (or other custom hooks) can itself be a custom hook. Custom hooks are the primary composition mechanism in React. They let you package a piece of state + the effects that manage it into a single, testable, reusable unit:
Custom hooks are just functions. They can be tested with React Testing Library's renderHook or by testing the component that uses them. They do not require mocking of component internals.
naming convention is enforced by the
ESLint plugin. The plugin uses the name to decide whether to apply the Rules of Hooks to a function call — if a function starts with
it is assumed to call hooks and must be called only at the top level of another hook or function component. A function that does not call hooks but is named with
will cause the plugin to warn if you call it conditionally.

Hooks are backward-compatible. React has no plans to remove class components. A codebase can mix class and function components freely — a class component can render a function component with hooks and vice versa. The React team's recommendation is to use hooks for new code, and to migrate class components opportunistically rather than all at once.
(defer expensive re-renders). These hooks express concurrent-mode concepts that have no class equivalent — they are hooks-only features.
Hooks were introduced to solve three structural problems in class components: reusing stateful logic (solved by custom hooks), grouping related side-effects together (solved by
), and eliminating class-specific complexity (solved by function components). A hook is any function starting with

that calls other hooks and runs only at the top level of a component or custom hook. They attach state to the component's Fiber via a stable-order linked list, which is why the Rules of Hooks are enforced.

The key insight: hooks did not add new capabilities to React. Function components can now do everything class components could, plus compose stateful logic through custom hooks in a way that was structurally impossible with classes. Everything else in the hooks API follows from these three design goals.
